segments.purgatorio-3-g30: Ora is an adverb meaning “now,” used here as a discourse marker to introduce what follows; the comma shows a pause and transition. It is the same word used for both 'hour' and 'now' in Italian, but here it functions temporally as 'now'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g31: This is a conditional clause introduced by se 'if' (here expressed in older diction). innanzi a me is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before/in front of me' (innanzi + a + me), placed before the verb for emphasis. nulla is the subject 'nothing', and s’aombra is an older reflexive/impersonal form (s’ = si) with the verb aombra (from ombrare) meaning 'casts a shadow / becomes shadowed' in the present indicative; the enclitic s’ appears before the vowel of aombra by poetic contraction.
segments.purgatorio-3-g32: non ti maravigliar is a negative imperative (don’t be astonished), with the clitic object ti 'you' attached to the verb. più che introduces a comparison 'more than'; here che functions as 'than' after più. d’i is the contracted form of di + i 'of the', so d’i cieli = 'at/of the heavens' (cieli in the sense of celestial spheres). The second che is a relative pronoun 'which' introducing the subordinate clause l’uno a l’altro raggio non ingombra, where l’uno a l’altro raggio literally 'one ray to the other' and ingombra (present indicative of ingombrare) means 'encumbers/obstructs'—so the heavens 'do not let one ray encumber another.' Note the poem's archaic spacing/elisions (l’ for il, a l’ for al) preserved in the syntax.
segments.purgatorio-3-g33: 'A' + infinitive expresses purpose here: 'A sofferir' = 'in order to suffer' (or 'to endure'). 'Sofferir' is the poetic infinitive form (older spelling) of soffrire, and 'tormenti' is the plural direct object 'torments'. The phrase sets the goal for what follows.
segments.purgatorio-3-g34: 'caldi e geli' are nominal/adjectival terms meaning 'the hot and the frosts' (heat and extreme cold); Dante places them before the noun for poetic effect. 'simili corpi' = 'similar bodies' (the objects being arranged). 'la Virtù dispone' is subject + present indicative verb: Virtù (here best understood as divine power/force) 'disposes' or 'arranges' these bodies. Word order is inverted (object phrase before verb) for rhythm.
segments.purgatorio-3-g35: 'come fa' is a short parenthetical clause: 'as it does' or 'in the way it acts/does.' 'Come' = 'as/like' and 'fa' is the present indicative of fare ('does/makes'), referring back to Virtù's action.
segments.purgatorio-3-g36: Negation + volere with a subordinate clause: 'non vuol che ...' = 'does not want that ...' The clitic 'ch’' = che introduces the subordinate; 'a noi' is the indirect object 'to us'; 'si sveli' is present subjunctive (sveli) with the reflexive/impersonal 'si' giving the sense 'be revealed' (i.e. 'reveal itself / be unveiled'). The whole means 'it does not wish that it be revealed to us.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g37: The sentence begins with a fronted predicate adjective 'Matto è' ('Mad is ...') followed by the relative clause 'chi spera ...' ('he who hopes ...'). 'Spera' is present indicative ('hopes'), and it governs a subordinate clause introduced by 'che' where 'nostra ragione' is the subject. 'Possa' is the present subjunctive of potere (used here because the clause depends on 'spera'), meaning 'may be able' or 'can.' 'Trascorrer' (poetic infinitive) means 'to pass through / traverse' the 'infinita via' ('infinite way/road'). The final relative clause 'che tiene una sustanza in tre persone' uses 'tiene' ('holds') to state that one substance is contained in three persons (a theological reference to the Trinity).
segments.purgatorio-3-g38: State is the plural imperative of stare here meaning 'be' (be in a state). contenti is an adjective (plural) meaning 'content' or 'satisfied' agreeing with the implied audience.
segments.purgatorio-3-g39: umana is an adjective 'human' qualifying gente; gente means 'people' and together address the human crowd ('human people').
segments.purgatorio-3-g40: al is the contraction a + il ('to the'/'at the') and combines with the following noun; quia is a Latin noun here meaning the word 'because' (the 'quia') — the phrase means 'be satisfied with the
segments.purgatorio-3-g41: ché is a conjunction 'for'/'because' introducing the reason that follows.
segments.purgatorio-3-g42: This is an archaic word order meaning 'if you had been able to see everything' — potuto is the past participle of potere ('been able'), aveste is the archaic second-person-plural auxiliary 'have' (here forming the compound past), and veder is the infinitive 'to see'; tutto is 'everything'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g43: mestier (mestiero) means 'need' or 'necessity'; non + era is the negative past copula 'there was not' (no need existed); parturir is the infinitive 'to give birth' (here used of Mary), and Maria is the proper name 'Mary'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g44: In this clause vedeste is the second-person-plural past tense 'you saw' and takes disïar (here a plural noun 'desires' in archaic spelling) as its object: 'and you saw desires without fruit'; sanza = 'without' and frutto = 'result/fruit'. tai is an archaic form meaning 'such' (tali) introducing the following result clause; che = 'that'; sarebbe is the conditional 'would be'; lor = 'to them/their'; disio = 'desire'; quetato = past participle 'quieted'/'calmed'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g45: ch’ is a shortened che 'that'; etternalmente = 'eternally' (adverb); è dato = 'is given' (present passive: è + past participle dato); lor = 'to them'; per here means 'as'/'for' in the sense 'given to them as (a) mourning'; lutto = 'mourning' or 'grief'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g46: io dico = 'I say' (present), followed by d’ Aristotile e di Plato e di molt’ altri = 'of/about Aristotle and Plato and many others' (di introduces the people named; molt’ is an apocopated form of molti = 'many').
segments.purgatorio-3-g47: e = 'and'; qui = 'here' or 'at this point'; chinò is the third-person singular past 'he bowed' (from chinare); la fronte = 'the forehead' (he bowed his head).
segments.purgatorio-3-g48: e più non disse = 'and he said no more' or 'said nothing further'; più non = 'no longer/any more' negating the verb disse (past 'said').
segments.purgatorio-3-g49: e rimase turbato = 'and he remained disturbed/troubled'; rimase is past 'remained' and turbato is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'disturbed' or 'agitated'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g50: Noi is the subject 'we'. divenimmo is the passato remoto of 'divenire' used here to mean 'we arrived/reached'; intanto ('meanwhile') follows the verb as a temporal adverb. a piè del monte is a prepositional phrase ('at the foot/base of the mountain'); piè is a poetic form for 'foot' and governs the article-del + noun.
segments.purgatorio-3-g51: quivi is a locative adverb meaning 'there'. trovammo is passato remoto 'we found'. la roccia is the direct object; sì is an intensifier meaning 'so' and erta (feminine) agrees with roccia to mean 'so steep'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g52: che introduces a result/consequence clause ('that'). ’ndarno is an adverb meaning 'in vain' modifying the whole clause. vi is locative 'there'; sarien is the conditional (archaic spelling of 'sarebbero') agreeing with gambe and meaning 'would be'; le gambe pronte ('the ready legs') is the clause's logical subject placed after the verb, with pronte agreeing in gender and number.
segments.purgatorio-3-g53: Tra Lerice e Turbìa is a prepositional phrase 'between Lerice and Turbia' naming locations; here Lerice and Turbìa are proper names (place-names). la più diserta is an attributive phrase 'the most deserted' placed before the noun that follows (poetic inversion).
segments.purgatorio-3-g54: la più rotta ruina is the subject phrase 'the most broken ruin' (superlative + adjective + noun). è una scala is the copular clause 'is a staircase', with una as the indefinite article and scala the predicate noun.
segments.purgatorio-3-g55: verso di quella is a directional phrase 'toward that (one/side)'; verso 'toward' takes the pronoun quella referring back to the ruin just named, with di introducing the demonstrative in this idiomatic construction.
segments.purgatorio-3-g56: agevole e aperta are two adjectives 'easy and open' describing the staircase (or its side); agevole is invariable for gender, aperta agrees feminine singular with scala/ruina, and the phrase is a succinct appraisal placed after the directional phrase.
segments.purgatorio-3-g57: Or ('now') opens the question. chi sa is a present-tense rhetorical question 'who knows'. da qual man uses man ('mano') figuratively as 'side' or 'hand' of the slope; la costa cala is a simple clause 'the slope descends' with cala in the present indicative.
segments.purgatorio-3-g58: disse is passato remoto 'he said'; ’l maestro mio is the subject 'my master' with the article elided to ’l. fermando is a present participle used circumstantially 'stopping' and + ’l passo ('his pace/step') indicates that he halted his movement while speaking.
segments.purgatorio-3-g59: sì che introduces a result or purpose clause 'so that'. possa is present subjunctive of potere used to express possibility or potential ('may be able'); salir is the infinitive 'to climb'. chi va sanz' ala is a relative/indefinite subject 'whoever goes without a wing' (sanz' = archaic contraction of 'senza'), and the subjunctive refers back to that subject.
segments.purgatorio-3-g60: This segment contains only a period (full stop) and no lexical material. Grammatically it functions as sentence-final punctuation marking the end of the utterance; there are no words to analyze for tense, mood, or meaning.
segments.purgatorio-3-g61: E = 'and' conjoining this clause to what precedes. 'mentre ch’' = 'while' (che introducing the subordinate temporal clause). 'e’' here is a contraction for egli (he) as the subject; it precedes the gerund. 'tenendo' is a gerund (present participle) meaning 'keeping' and forms a circumstantial phrase 'tenendo ’l viso basso' = 'keeping his face low'. 'essaminava' is the imperfect of essaminare (narrative ongoing past) meaning 'was examining/considering'. The object phrase is inverted: 'del cammin la mente' = 'la mente del cammin' literally 'the mind of the path' = 'the thought/plan of the path' (i.e. the course of the path) — Dante freely inverts word order for meter.
segments.purgatorio-3-g62: 'e io mirava' = 'and I was looking' with mirava in the imperfect (ongoing past). 'suso' = 'up' or 'upward'. 'intorno al sasso' = 'around the rock' (intorno governs the prepositional phrase 'al sasso'); note the simple preposition+article contraction 'al'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g63: 'da man sinistra' = lit. 'from (the) left hand/side' — 'da' marks source or position and 'man sinistra' = 'left-hand side' (Dante often omits final vowels). 'm’apparì' is the compound verb 'apparìre' with clitic 'mi' contracted to m' — 'appeared to me' (passive/impersonal sense). 'una gente d’anime' = 'a people/company of souls' (una gente = a company; d’ = di 'of').
segments.purgatorio-3-g64: 'che' is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the clause describing the people. 'movieno' is the imperfect plural (they were moving) describing ongoing motion. 'i piè' = 'the feet' (direct object); 'ver’ noi' is an abbreviated form of 'verso noi' = 'toward us' (here 'ver’' = verso).
segments.purgatorio-3-g65: 'e non pareva' = 'and it did not appear/seem' with pareva in the imperfect; this is a comment on the preceding action (their approach did not seem a certain way). The clause is elliptical — Dante omits a conjunction like 'che' that might be expected in modern prose.
segments.purgatorio-3-g66: 'sì' here = 'so' or 'in that way' (not 'yes'). 'venïan' (vegnian) is the imperfect subjunctive/indicative form meaning 'they were coming' (Dante retains the i-diaeresis in printing; functionally imperfect). 'lente' = 'slow(ly)'; word order places adverb after verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-3-g67: Imperative singular of levare (to raise/lift). As a one-word command it takes an implied object (the eyes mentioned immediately after). This is a direct vocative command addressed to the master.
segments.purgatorio-3-g68: "diss'" is an archaic, contracted poetic form corresponding to 'dissi' or 'disse' (I said) in the narrative past; the subject pronoun 'io' is explicit after the verb, a poetic inversion for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-3-g69: Vocative noun addressing the guide: 'maestro' = 'master/teacher'. In Italian the vocative uses the same form as the nominative; here it directly follows the verb of speech.
segments.purgatorio-3-g70: Li is the old masculine plural definite article ('the'); occhi (plural) is the noun 'eyes'; tuoi is the possessive 'your'. The possessive follows the noun (occhi tuoi) as is common in older/poetic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-3-g71: Ecco introduces the presence of someone ('here is'). Di qua means 'on this side' or 'from here'. Chi is an indefinite/relative pronoun 'who' meaning 'someone who'. Ne is the clitic referring back to the matter at hand ('about it' / 'for us'); darà is future indicative 'will give'; consiglio is the direct object 'counsel/advice'. Word order is natural: 'here is who will give us counsel (about it)'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g72: Se is the conditional 'if' (introducing the subordinate conditional clause). Tu is the subject pronoun 'you'. Da te medesmo is a reflexive/source phrase 'from/by yourself' (da + reflexive pronoun + medesmo 'selfsame'). Aver is the infinitive 'to have'; nol is an archaic contracted clitic (non + lo) meaning 'not it/him' — here 'not it' (i.e. 'it not'); puoi is present indicative 'you can'. The clause thus means 'if you cannot have it from yourself.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g73: Guardò is passato prossimo/remote (passato remoto) third-person singular of guardare: 'he looked/turned his gaze'. Allora is an adverb 'then' marking the time of the action.
segments.purgatorio-3-g74: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Con libero piglio is a prepositional phrase ('with a free/composed bearing'); piglio here means 'bearing/manner/air' and libero qualifies it. Rispuose is an archaic spelling of 'rispose', passato remoto third-person singular 'he answered/replied'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g75: Andiamo is first-person plural present/hortative 'let us go' (or simply 'we go' in context). In là is an adverbial phrase 'over there / in that direction'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g76: Ch' is a contraction of che (that/because). Ei is an archaic third-person plural pronoun 'they'. Vegnon is an archaic present form of 'vengono' (they come). Piano here is an adverb meaning 'slowly / gently'. The clause gives the reason: 'for they are coming slowly.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g77: E 'and'. Tu is the vocative/subject 'you'. Ferma is an imperative (second person singular) of fermare meaning 'steady/hold/keep (back)'; here it governs the object that follows. La spene is an archaic noun phrase for 'the hope' (la = the, spene = hope).
segments.purgatorio-3-g78: Dolce is an adjective 'sweet/dear' used as a term of endearment; figlio is vocative 'son'. Together they form a tender address: 'sweet son.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g79: Ancora is an adverb 'still/yet'. Era is the imperfect (or narrative past) third-person singular of essere 'was'. Quel popol is the demonstrative plus noun 'that crowd/people'. Di lontano is a prepositional phrase 'from afar' indicating distance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g80: I' dico is a parenthetical 'I say' (the contraction i' stands for io in poetic orthography) used to clarify the speaker's measure; dico is present 'I say'. Dopo i nostri mille passi is a temporal/distance phrase 'after our thousand steps.' Note the archaic contraction i' and the sequence measuring distance by steps.
segments.purgatorio-3-g81: Quanto here functions as a comparative 'as much as / to the extent that'. Un buon gittator is 'a good thrower/hurler' (gittator = someone who throws). Trarria is the conditional third-person singular of trarre/trar (would draw/pull or would reach); con mano 'with (the) hand' specifies the measure (how far a thrower would reach with his hand). This is a simile measuring distance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g82: Quando is 'when'. Si is the reflexive/clitic marker (they themselves). Strinser is an archaic past form (strinser[o]/strinsero) meaning 'pressed together' or 'closed in'; here reflexive: 'they pressed themselves together'. Tutti 'all'. Ai duri massi = a + i 'to the hard boulders'; de l'alta ripa = archaic 'della alta ripa' (of the high bank). The clause describes their crowding to the rocks.
segments.purgatorio-3-g83: E 'and'. Stetter is an archaic past form (stetter[o]/stettero) of stare: 'they stood' or 'remained'. Fermi e stretti are predicates 'firm and close/tight' describing their posture. Com' a guardar is a construction 'as (if) to watch' or 'as if watching' (com' = come 'as', plus a + infinitive guardar).
segments.purgatorio-3-g84: Chi is the indefinite/relative pronoun 'who/one who'. Va dubbiando is a periphrastic expression: va (goes) with the gerund dubbiando ('doubting/hesitating'), meaning 'goes along hesitating' or 'hesitates as he goes'. This clause specifies the kind of person being described in the comparison.
segments.purgatorio-3-g85: Stassi is an archaic subjunctive/optative form of stare meaning 'would stay' / 'should stay' / 'would stand' in a hypothetical or descriptive comparison; here it completes the simile ('as one who goes hesitating would stay').
segments.purgatorio-3-g16: 'Lo' is the archaic masculine definite article (modern il/lo) placed before the noun; 'sol' here is the noun 'sole' (the sun) in an older form rather than the adverb 'only'. Together they mean 'the sun'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g17: Relative clause introduced by 'che' ('which') describing the sun. 'dietro' = 'behind'; 'fiammeggiava' is the imperfect of 'fiammeggiare' meaning 'was blazing/gleaming' (progressive past); 'roggio' is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning a reddish/crimson color — an unusual form, so its precise lexical sense is marked uncertain.
segments.purgatorio-3-g18: 'rotto' is a past participle meaning 'broken' or 'interrupted'; the sequence 'rotto m'era' uses the participle with the auxiliary 'era' (imperfect of essere) giving a verbal periphrasis 'had been/was broken' as perceived by the speaker. 'm’' is the clitic 'mi' ('to me' / 'for me'); 'dinanzi a la figura' = 'in front of the figure' (dinanzi + a + article + noun). Word order is typical Dantean placement of participle + clitic + auxiliary.
segments.purgatorio-3-g19: Relative clause continuing the description: 'ch’' = 'che' ('which'); 'avëa' is the imperfect of 'avere' (archaic spelling with diaeresis) = 'had'; 'in me' = 'in/within me' (i.e., on me); 'de’' = contraction of 'dei'/'di' + article (here 'of the'); 'suoi raggi' = 'its/his rays'; 'l’appoggio' = 'the support/resting place' (literal 'the support' of the rays). The clause says the figure received/used the rays' support upon the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-3-g20: 'Io mi volsi' = 'I turned' (volsi = past historic of volgere/volgere → 'turned'); 'dallato' is an older/dialectal form meaning 'to the side' (literally 'to the side' rather than 'altar' in the lemma list) — here it indicates turning aside. 'con paura' = 'with fear'; 'd’essere abbandonato' = 'of being abandoned' (di + infinitive/passive participle functioning as the object of 'paura').
segments.purgatorio-3-g21: 'quand’ io vidi' = 'when I saw' (quand' = quando; vidi = past of vedere). 'solo' modifies 'la terra' meaning 'the land alone' or 'only the land'; 'dinanzi a me' = 'in front of me' (dinanzi + a + pronoun). 'la terra oscura' = 'the dark/obscure ground', with 'oscura' as an adjective in agreement.
segments.purgatorio-3-g22: 'e' = 'and'; '’l' is the elided form of 'il' ('the'); 'mio' = 'my'; 'conforto' = 'comfort' or 'consolation' — here it introduces the comforting speech that follows.
segments.purgatorio-3-g23: Direct quotation: 'Perché' = 'why' (here as 'why do you'); 'pur' (from 'pure') = 'still' / 'even now'; 'diffidi' is present indicative of 'diffidare' meaning 'you distrust / you mistrust'. The line is the speaker's consolation asking rhetorically why he still doubts.
segments.purgatorio-3-g24: This segment is a comma/pause in the verse; it marks a break before the reported speech and has no lexical content.
segments.purgatorio-3-g25: 'a dir mi cominciò' is an inverted word order meaning 'began to say to me' (cominciò = began, a + infinitive = began to ...; 'dir mi' places the clitic object after the infinitive, a common poetic order). 'tutto rivolto' = 'wholly turned' (tutto as adverb = completely; 'rivolto' = turned toward), describing the speaker's orientation toward the narrator as he begins speaking.
segments.purgatorio-3-g26: Reported question: 'non credi tu me teco' = 'do you not believe that I am with you' (non = not; credi = you believe; 'me teco' = me with you = 'that I am with you'). 'e ch’io ti guidi' = 'and that I may guide you' — 'ch’' = che ('that'); 'io' = subject pronoun for emphasis; 'ti' = you (object); 'guidi' is present subjunctive (che + subjunctive) used in subordinate clause expressing the idea 'that I guide you' — a common Dantean/poetic sequence. Overall a rhetorical reassurance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g27: 'Vespero' = 'evening' (or vespers) as a time-marker; 'è già colà' = 'is already there' (è = is; già = already; colà = there). 'dov’ è sepolto lo corpo' = 'where the body is buried' (dov' = dove = where; sepolto = buried). 'dentro al quale io facea ombra' = 'inside which I used to cast a shadow' — 'facea' is the imperfect of fare (archaic spelling), meaning 'I made/was making' and indicates habitual or continuous action in the past; 'ombra' = 'shadow'. The whole clause explains the location and the narrator's relation to the buried body (he was its shade).
segments.purgatorio-3-g28: 'Napoli l’ha' = 'Naples has it' (Napoli = subject; 'l’' = 'lo' = it, referring back to 'il corpo'; 'ha' = has). Short, direct statement assigning possession.
segments.purgatorio-3-g29: 'e' = 'and'; 'da Brandizio' indicates the agent/author ('by Brandizio' or 'from Brandizio'); 'è tolto' uses 'essere' + past participle 'tolto' (from togliere) giving a passive or resultative meaning 'has been taken/removed' — the phrase says the body has been taken away by Brandizio. 'Brandizio' here is a proper name referenced as agent.
segments.purgatorio-3-g168: Vedi is an imperative (2nd person singular) meaning “see” or “consider.” Oggimai is an adverb meaning “now/by now” placed after the verb for emphasis. The clause se tu mi puoi far lieto is a conditional subordinate: se (“if”) introduces the condition, tu is an explicit subject pronoun for emphasis, mi is the clitic object “me” placed before the finite verb puoi (present indicative, 2nd sing. of potere, “you can”), which is followed by the bare infinitive far (fare) and the adjective lieto (“happy”) as predicate complement.
segments.purgatorio-3-g169: Revelando is a gerund (present participle) meaning “revealing” that expresses the manner or content by which the speaker would be made happy. A introduces the indirect object la mia buona Costanza (“to my good Constance”): la is the definite article, mia and buona are possessive and adjective modifying the proper name Costanza. Come begins the reported content clause “how,” and m’hai visto is a contraction of mi hai visto (mi + auxiliary avere + past participle visto), i.e. the present perfect meaning “you have seen me” (here rendered simply “you saw me” in natural English).
segments.purgatorio-3-g170: E anco esto divieto links another item to be revealed: e (“and”) + anco (archaic for anche, “also”) introduce esto (archaic form of questo, “this”) modifying divieto (“prohibition, interdiction”). The noun is the direct object of the preceding gerundal action (revelando). Note the archaic spelling esto instead of modern questo.
segments.purgatorio-3-g171: Ché is a conjunction meaning “for/because” introducing the reason. Qui means “here,” and per quei di là is a prepositional phrase literally “because/for those from over there” (per + quei + di + là), referring to people on the other side; per carries the sense “because of/for.” Molto is “much,” and s’avanza is the impersonal or reflexive si + avanza (3rd sing. present of avanzare) meaning roughly “is left/remains” or “there is much (that remains/that is to be had).”
segments.purgatorio-3-g1: Avvegna che is a concessive formula (literally “it happens that”) meaning “although/even if,” introducing a subordinate clause that takes the imperfect-subjunctive dispergesse. Dispergesse is the imperfect subjunctive of dispergere (“to scatter”), and color is an old plural demonstrative/collective (“those [people]”); per la campagna = “through the countryside.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g2: Rivolti is a past participle used predicatively (“turned”) agreeing with the people just mentioned; al monte = “toward the mountain.” Ove is a relative/adverbial “where,” and ragion fruga is literal “reason rummages/searches” (fruga is present indicative), so this relative clause says the mountain is a place “where reason searches/probes.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g3: I’ is the poet’s contracted form of io (“I”); mi is the reflexive clitic (“myself”/“to myself”), and ristrinsi is passato remoto first person singular of ristrinsare (“I drew myself in / contracted / drew close”). A la fida compagna is a prepositional phrase “to the faithful companion,” so the whole means “I drew myself close to my faithful companion.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g4: E come introduces a rhetorical question (“and how…”). Sare’ is a poetic contraction of the conditional saresti/sarei of essere (“would be”/auxiliary), combined with the past participle corso to form a conditional perfect sense (“would I have run” / “would I have been able to run”). Sanza = “without,” and lui = “him.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g5: Chi is the interrogative “who.” M’avria is the enclitic form of mi avrebbe (conditional perfect “would have [to me]”); tratto is the past participle of trarre (“pulled/drawn”), and su = “up.” Per la montagna indicates the route or place (“up the mountain”). The whole is a simple conditional perfect interrogative: “Who would have pulled me up the mountain?”
segments.purgatorio-3-g6: El is the archaic/hemi-dialectal form of egli/il (“he/it”); mi is the dative clitic “to me”; parea is the imperfect of parere (“seemed”); da sé stesso = “of/from himself” (emphatic reflexive); rimorso = “remorse.” Grammatically this is an imperfect-appearance statement: “He seemed to me like remorse issuing from himself.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g7: O here is an exclamative/interjective particle ("oh"/“or”), introducing an alternative or apposition to the preceding notion. Dignitosa and netta are adjectives modifying coscïenza (“conscience”), so this phrase apposes or contrasts “rimorso” with “a dignified and clear conscience.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g8: Come t’è is an exclamatory phrase (“how to you is…” = “how…you are…”); t’ is the clitic for ti (“to you”), and è is present of essere. Picciol fallo = “small fault,” and amaro morso = “bitter bite/pang” (morso as a noun). The line is an exclamation: “How that small fault is a bitter bite to you!”
segments.purgatorio-3-g9: Quando introduces a temporal clause “when.” Li piedi suoi = “his feet” (li = definite article + piedi = subject, suoi = possessive). Lasciar is infinitive ‘to leave’ used here poetically in place of a finite past (“left off”), so the sense is “when his feet left off their haste.” La fretta = “the haste.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g10: Che = “which” (relative) referring back to the leaving of haste; l’onestade here means “honor/propriety.” Ad ogn’ atto = “at every action/gesture,” and dismaga (3rd person present) means “takes away/disheartens/diminishes.” Grammatically this subordinate clause explains the moral effect of abandoning haste.
segments.purgatorio-3-g11: La mente mia is a nominative noun phrase (“my mind”) and serves as the subject of the following clauses; position before the relative clause is a normal poetic/Italian order emphasizing the possessor mio.
segments.purgatorio-3-g12: Che prima era ristretta is a relative clause modifying la mente mia: che = “which,” prima = “before/previously,” era = imperfect of essere (“was”), ristretta = past participle/adjective meaning “contracted/narrowed.” Together: “which before had been narrowed.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g13: Lo ’ntento is the contracted form of l’intento (“the intent/aim”) where the initial vowel is dropped and marked by an apostrophe; rallargò is passato remoto (3rd person) of rallargare (“widened/expanded”), so “the intent widened.” The verb is coordinated to the previous subject (la mente mia).
segments.purgatorio-3-g14: Sì come = “just as / even as.” Vaga here is either an adjective “wandering” or a present verb (“[it] wanders”) agreeing with the feminine idea (mente/intent) — in either reading it means “as it wanders” (i.e., as the mind wanders).
segments.purgatorio-3-g15: E diedi ’l viso mio = “and I turned/gave my face” (diedi, passato remoto of dare, used idiomatically for turning the face). Incontr’ al poggio = contracted incontro “toward/opposite” the hill (incontr’ = incontro). Che ’nverso ’l ciel più alto si dislaga is a relative clause modifying poggio: che = “which,” ’nverso (in verso) = “toward,” ’l ciel più alto = “the higher heaven/sky,” si dislaga = reflexive/present “spreads itself out / extends.” So: “and I turned my face toward the hill which spreads itself up toward the higher heaven.”
segments.purgatorio-3-g129: Quand' is the contracted form of quando (when). The clause uses the compound past with the auxiliary fui + past participle disdetto: mi (reflexive) precedes the auxiliary (mi fui) and umilmente is an adverb modifying the verb; disdetto (retracted/denied) takes the infinitival complement introduced by di + averlo visto (to have seen him), where the clitic lo attaches to the infinitive averlo. Mai modifies visto (ever).
segments.purgatorio-3-g130: El is an archaic subject pronoun (= egli/he) and disse is passato remoto, third person singular of dire — 'he said.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g131: Or means 'now' and vedi is a present/imperative second-person form of vedere used as an exhortation in direct speech: 'look' or 'see.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g132: The coordinating e = 'and'. Mostrommi is passato remoto mostrò with the clitic mi attached (he showed me). Una piaga = 'a wound'; a sommo 'l petto is an idiomatic locution: a + sommo (at the top/highest part) + el (’l) + petto = 'at the top of his chest.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g133: Poi is a temporal connector 'then' and sorridendo is a gerund ('smiling') functioning adverbially; disse is passato remoto 'he said.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g134: Io son is the archaic present of essere (I am); Manfredi is the proper name. Son is the short form of sono used in older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-3-g135: Nepote here means a descendant/relative (the kinship term), governed by di + Costanza (the name) in apposition to imperadrice (empress). The phrase names his relation to Empress Constance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g136: Ond' is a contraction of 'onde'/'ond' meaning 'therefore' or 'wherefore'; io ti priego is a present-tense entreaty ('I beg you'); che introduces the subordinate clause.
segments.purgatorio-3-g137: Quando introduces a temporal clause 'when'; tu is the subject; riedi is an older-form present (or present with future reference) meaning 'you return/go back' (second-person singular).
segments.purgatorio-3-g138: Vadi is an archaic imperative/present form meaning 'go' (second person singular) used here as the directive in the request; a + mia bella figlia is the indirect object 'to my fair daughter.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g139: Genitrice is a noun 'mother/originator' used appositionally to describe the daughter as the source of honor; de l' is the elided form of 'dell'' (of the); onor di Cicilia e d'Aragona = 'honor of Sicily and of Aragon.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g140: E = 'and'; dichi is an archaic imperative/second-person form of dire meaning 'tell' or 'say' (note the clitic '’l' attached before the noun: dichi 'l vero = 'tell the truth'); vero = 'truth'; a lei = 'to her.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g141: S' (here a contraction of se) introduces a conditional 'if' — s'altro si dice = 'if anything else is said.' Si dice is the impersonal/passive construction 'is said.' Altro = 'other/anything else.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g142: Poscia ch' = 'after that'; ebbi is passato remoto of avere used here to form a perfect construction ('I had'); rotta (feminine past participle) agrees with la persona ('the body/person'); di due punte mortali = 'by two mortal points' (punte = points/spear tips, mortali = deadly). The whole phrase expresses a prior, completed action.
segments.purgatorio-3-g143: Io mi rendei: io (I) + reflexive mi + rendei (passato remoto of rendere) here means 'I yielded/surrendered myself' or 'I gave myself up.' The reflexive marks that the subject hands himself over.
segments.purgatorio-3-g144: Piangendo is a gerund 'weeping,' giving the manner in which he yielded himself (adverbial modifier).
segments.purgatorio-3-g145: A = 'to'; quei = 'that one/those' used here homage to God ('to Him'); che = 'who'; volontier = 'willingly/gladly'; perdona = present tense third-person singular of perdonare = 'forgives' or 'pardons.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g146: Orribil is an adjective placed before the verb for emphasis; furon is passato remoto (they were). Li peccati miei = 'my sins' with the definite article li and possessive miei.
segments.purgatorio-3-g147: Ma = 'but' (contrast). La bontà infinita = 'infinite goodness' (adjective infinita modifying bontà). Ha is present of avere 'has'; sì gran braccia = 'so great arms' — a metaphor: mercy has broad/strong arms.
segments.purgatorio-3-g148: Che is the relative pronoun 'that'; prende = present-indicative 'takes' or 'accepts'; ciò che = 'what/that which'; si rivolge is a reflexive present 'turns itself' or 'turns toward' (si marks the reflexive/impersonal voice); a lei = 'to it/her' referencing bontà (feminine), so the relative clause means 'that accepts whatever turns to it.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g114: Non vi maravigliate is a negative command addressed to the listeners (vi = you, plural). Non + verb here gives the prohibitive sense 'do not marvel' (an older/poetic imperative/subjunctive form of maravigliare). Word order places the negation first for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-3-g115: Ma credete is an exhortation 'but believe' (credete here functions as an imperative plural). The clause introduced by che is a subordinate content clause. Non sanza virtù che da ciel vegna is an older/poetic expression meaning 'not without a virtue/power which comes from heaven' (sanza = senza, vegna = archaic form of venire 'comes'). Cerchi di soverchiar questa parete uses cerchi (archaic present form 'seeks/tries') + di + infinitive soverchiar 'to overpower', meaning 'tries to overpower this wall'; the subject is implicit.
segments.purgatorio-3-g116: Così 'l maestro is a short narrative clause: Così = 'thus/so', and ’l is an elided article (il) before maestro. The phrase reports what the master (Virgil) had said or done immediately before.
segments.purgatorio-3-g117: E quella gente degna introduces the speakers: e = 'and', quella gente = 'that people/group', degna = 'worthy' qualifying the group. Tornate is an imperative plural ('Return!' or 'Go back!') addressed to the addressees.
segments.purgatorio-3-g118: Disse is the past-tense verb 'said' (dire) reporting speech; it stands between the two quoted utterances as the narrative marker.
segments.purgatorio-3-g119: «intrate innanzi dunque» is a sequence of imperatives/adverbs: intrate (imperative plural 'enter/come in'), innanzi = 'forward/ahead' or 'before', and dunque = 'then/therefore' (here temporal/sequence 'then'). Together they mean 'enter forward, then.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g120: Coi dossi de le man faccendo insegna is an ablative/instrumental phrase: coi = con + il 'with the', dossi (plural of dosso) = 'backs' (here 'backs of the hands'), de = di 'of', le man = 'the hands'. Faccendo is a gerund/participle 'making' and insegna = 'a sign' (here a bodily gesture). The whole phrase means 'making a sign with the backs of their hands.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g121: E un di loro incominciò is narrative: e = 'and', un di loro = 'one of them', and incominciò is past 'began' (incominciare). It introduces the following direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-3-g122: «Chiunque tu se’ is the opening of direct speech: Chiunque = 'whoever/anyone', tu = 'you', se' = archaic form of sei ('are')—so 'whoever you are.' Note the archaic second-person form se' instead of modern sei.
segments.purgatorio-3-g123: Così andando is a circumstantial participial phrase: così = 'thus/so/as such', andando = present participle of andare 'going/passing by', i.e. 'as you go on' or 'as you pass'. It modifies the imperative that follows.
segments.purgatorio-3-g124: Volgi ’l viso is an imperative: volgi (archaic/poetic form of volgere) = 'turn' (2nd person singular imperative here), ’l = the (il) and viso = 'face'. Together: 'turn your face.'
segments.purgatorio-3-g125: Pon mente is an idiom meaning 'pay attention' or 'notice' (pon = put/place, mente = mind). Here it introduces a clause of caution: se di là mi vedesti unque = 'if/whether you ever saw me there' (unque = 'ever/at any time'). Note: the provided lemma-sense lists for se and vedesti do not match their usage here (se = 'if/whether', vedesti = 'saw'), so those are marked unknown.
segments.purgatorio-3-g126: Io mi volsi ver’ lui e guardail fiso is a sequence of past-tense actions: Io mi volsi = 'I turned myself' (volsi = passato of volgere), ver' = contracted verso 'toward' (archaic), lui = 'him'. Guardail is an enclitic/contracted past form meaning 'I looked at him' (guardai + l), and fiso = 'fixed/firmly' (adverbial). Together: 'I turned toward him and looked fixedly at him.' Note archaic contractions (ver', guardail).
segments.purgatorio-3-g127: Biondo era e bello e di gentile aspetto is a descriptive clause with era (was) as the copula. Biondo = 'blond', bello = 'handsome/beautiful', and di gentile aspetto = 'of noble/highborn/courteous appearance' (gentile here = noble/highborn in older Italian). Word order places adjectives before the copula for a poetic listing.
segments.purgatorio-3-g128: Ma l’un de’ cigli un colpo avea diviso is a clause giving a contrast (ma = but). L’un de' cigli = 'one of the brows/eyelashes/edges' (here 'one of his eyebrows/eye-edges'), un colpo = 'a blow', avea diviso = pluperfect/perfect-ish construction 'had split/divided'. The verb avea (had) + past participle diviso expresses a past state resulting from that blow.
segments.purgatorio-3-g149: Se introduces a conditional ('if'). The article l' is the contracted il before a vowel. Pastor di Cosenza is a nominal phrase 'the shepherd of Cosenza' (city as qualifier). Word order is normal: conditional protasis begins here.
segments.purgatorio-3-g150: che is a relative pronoun ('who/which') introducing the clause that modifies 'pastor'. a la caccia di me fu messo is a passive construction: fu (past of essere) + messo (past participle) = 'was put/assigned to the hunt for me'. per Clemente allora gives the agent/author ('by Clement then').
segments.purgatorio-3-g151: avesse ... letta is a past subjunctive (trapassato subjunctive) used in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional: 'had read'. in Dio here means 'in God' or 'by God's aid' (a phrase indicating divine knowledge). ben modifies letta = 'well/read correctly/discerned'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g152: sarieno is the archaic conditional form (would be; modern sarebbe) agreeing with the plural subject l'ossa; ancora = 'still'. in co del ponte is an older locution: in co (in the hollow/arch/span) + del ponte = 'in the arch/hollow of the bridge'. presso a Benevento = 'near Benevento'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g153: sotto la guardia = 'under the guard/custody'; de la is the older form of di + la. grave is an adjective meaning 'serious/weighty/strict' modifying mora; mora here is the sense 'Moorish woman' (a person). The whole phrase gives the custodian under whose watch the bones lie.
segments.purgatorio-3-g154: Or = 'now'. le is the feminine plural object pronoun referring back to 'ossa' ('them'). bagna and move are present-tense verbs ('wets' and 'stirs/moves'); la pioggia and il vento are the agents. di fuor dal regno = 'from outside the kingdom/realm'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g155: quasi modifies lungo, giving 'almost along' (nearly along). lungo 'along' governs the (proper) name Verde (here a place or stream), so the phrase means 'almost along the Verde'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g156: dov' = dove 'where'. e' here is an archaic clitic for egli ('he') acting as the subject; le is the object pronoun 'them'. trasmutò is passato remoto 'he changed/transferred'. a lume spento literally 'by extinguished light' = 'under cover of darkness' or 'at night with the light out'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g157: Per lor maladizion = 'by/through their curse'. sì modifies the negated verb phrase to mean 'so (much)' (i.e. 'not so lost'). si perde is a reflexive/impersonal construction meaning 'is lost' or 'perishes'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g158: che introduces a result/complement clause: 'that it cannot return'. possa is present subjunctive of potere used after che to express potentiality in this negative/result construction; tornar is the infinitive 'to return'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g159: l’etterno amore is the noun phrase resumed after the clause: 'the eternal love'. The article + adjective + noun is straightforward; placement after the clause is topical.
segments.purgatorio-3-g160: mentre che = 'while/so long as'. la speranza ha fior del verde is idiomatic: 'hope has a flower (bloom) of green' — i.e. hope still bears the green bud, meaning it is still alive/fresh. ha is present 'has'; fior = 'bloom/flower' used metaphorically.
segments.purgatorio-3-g161: Vero è che = 'It is true that'. quale functions as 'whoever' (an indefinite relative). in contumacia more = 'dies in contumacy' (more is an archaic form of muore = 'dies'); di Santa Chiesa = 'against/of the Holy Church' (i.e. contumacy toward the Church).
segments.purgatorio-3-g162: ancor ch'al fin si penta = 'even though at the end he repents'; ancor ch' is concessive ('even if/though'), al fin = 'at the end', si penta = reflexive 'he repents'. The clause concedes possible late repentance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g163: star li convien = 'they must remain' / 'it is necessary that they stay': star (infinitive) + convien (impersonal 'it is necessary') with li pointing to the persons concerned. da questa ripa in fore = 'off/outside this bank'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g164: per ognun tempo ch'elli è stato = 'for each period of time that he has been' (ch'elli = che egli, archaic subject form). The clause sets the measure over which the following numeral applies.
segments.purgatorio-3-g165: trenta is the numeral 'thirty' attached to the previous measure: it indicates the quantity applied to each time/period mentioned (historically understood as years; Dante leaves the unit implicit).
segments.purgatorio-3-g166: in sua presunzïon = 'in his presumption' — a qualifying phrase indicating that the thirty (units) are counted with regard to the person's presumption/arrogance.
segments.purgatorio-3-g167: se tal decreto più corto per buon prieghi non diventa is a conditional/exception: 'unless such a decree is not made shorter by good prayers' (i.e. 'unless the decree is shortened by good prayers'). per buon prieghi = 'through good prayers'; diventa is present 'becomes/turns'.
segments.purgatorio-3-g86: "O" is the vocative interjection (oh); "ben finiti" is an adjective phrase where "ben" (an adverb) modifies the past participle "finiti" used adjectivally (= "well finished/complete"). "Finiti" is plural masculine to agree with the implied addressees (spirits).
segments.purgatorio-3-g87: The second vocative "o" repeats the address; "già" is the adverb "already"; "spiriti eletti" is a noun phrase: "spiriti" (spirits) and "eletti" the past participle used adjectivally meaning "chosen/elected." The phrase addresses those souls who have already been chosen/completed.
segments.purgatorio-3-g88: "Virgilio" is the nominative subject; "incominciò" is passato remoto 3rd singular of "incominciare" (he began) — the simple past typical of epic/narrative style.
segments.purgatorio-3-g89: "per quella pace" = "for/with regard to that peace"; the clause "ch’i’ credo" is a parenthetical relative/subordinate with an emphatic archaic subject pronoun "i’" (I) + "credo" (present "I believe"). After that, "che per voi tutti s’aspetti" contains the clitic "s'" (si) plus the present subjunctive "aspetti" because it’s subordinate to a verb of belief: here it functions impersonally/passively as "is expected" (i.e. "that is expected for all of you").
segments.purgatorio-3-g90: "ditene" is an imperative form of "dire" with an enclitic (old/poetic spelling) meaning "tell us/tell me of it"; it governs the following indirect question "dove la montagna giace" — "where the mountain lies." "Giace" is present tense meaning "lies/ is situated."
segments.purgatorio-3-g91: "sì che" introduces a result/purpose clause ("so that"); "possibil" is an archaic adjectival form meaning "possible/feasible"; "sia" is present subjunctive of "essere" used to express possibility ("that it may be"); "l'andare" is a nominalized infinitive "the going/upward movement"; "in suso" is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning "upwards."
segments.purgatorio-3-g92: "ché" here is a causal conjunction (= "for/because"); the infinitive phrase "perder tempo" (= "to lose/waste time") functions as the theme of the clause; "a chi più sa più spiace" is an impersonal/ethical-dative construction: "it is more displeasing to whoever knows more" — the doubled "più...più" is a comparative pattern (the more... the more...). "Spiace" is present indicative (it displeases).
segments.purgatorio-3-g93: "Come" = "as/like"; "le pecorelle" is the diminutive plural "little sheep"; "escon" is an archaic poetic form of "escono" (present indicative, 3rd pl.: "they go out/leave"); "del chiuso" = "from the fold/enclosure"; "a una" = "one at a time/one by one."
segments.purgatorio-3-g94: Continuation of the listing: "a due" functions adverbially meaning "in twos" or "two by two"; the preposition "a" plus a numeral expresses grouping.
segments.purgatorio-3-g95: Same pattern: "a tre" = "in threes/three by three," parallel to the previous items.
segments.purgatorio-3-g96: "e l’altre stanno timidette" = "and the others stand timidly/shyly"; "stanno" is present of "stare" (they stand/are); "timidette" (diminutive/pejorative) describes their shy demeanor; "atterrando l’occhio e ’l muso" uses the present participle "atterrando" (= "casting down/lowering") applied to eyes and muzzles, i.e. they lower their gaze and faces.
segments.purgatorio-3-g97: "e ciò che fa la prima," = "and that which the first does" — "ciò che" is the correlative "that which/what" and "fa" is 3rd singular present of "fare" (does).
segments.purgatorio-3-g98: "e l’altre fanno," = "and the others do (the same)" — simple present plural "fanno." The clause shows direct imitation: what the first does, the rest replicate.
segments.purgatorio-3-g99: "addossandosi a lei," is a gerund phrase (present gerund of "addossare" with reflexive clitic) meaning "pressing/leaning themselves against her." The gerund indicates a simultaneous or accompanying action to what the others do.
segments.purgatorio-3-g100: The sequence "s’ella s’arresta," contains an archaic elision: the first "s’" stands for "se" (if), "ella" is the subject "she," and the second "s’" is the reflexive clitic of "si arresta" (she stops). The whole means "if she halts/stops."
segments.purgatorio-3-g101: "semplici e quete," are predicates (plural, feminine) describing the animals as "simple/plain" and "quiet/calm." They agree with "altre" (the ewes/others).
segments.purgatorio-3-g102: In "e lo ’mperché non sanno;" the archaic phrase "lo 'mperché" (= "the why/the reason") is the object: "and they do not know the reason." "Sanno" is present of "sapere."
segments.purgatorio-3-g103: "sì vid’ io muovere a venir la testa di quella mandra fortunata allotta," — "vid'" is the apocopated passato remoto "vidi" (I saw); the construction "vidi ... muovere" uses the verb of perception plus an infinitive to mean "I saw ... move/begin to move." "a venir" = "to come"; "la testa di quella mandra" = "the head of that flock"; "fortunata" is an adjectival epithet (fortunate/lucky); "allotta" is an adverb meaning roughly "thereupon/at once/together," an archaic connective indicating movement as a unit.
segments.purgatorio-3-g104: "pudica in faccia e ne l’andare onesta." — "pudica" (modest/chaste) modifies the head/flock; "in faccia" = "in (its) face/countenance"; "ne l'andare" (archaic "in the going") = "in its gait/way of moving"; "onesta" = "honest/modest/upright". Overall: modest in countenance and modest/upright in its bearing.
segments.purgatorio-3-g105: "Come" = "as"; "color dinanzi" = "those in front" ("color" is an archaic pronoun for "those"), "vider" is the archaic passato remoto "videro/they saw"; "rotta la luce in terra" = "the light broken on the ground"; "dal mio destro canto" = "from my right side/quarter" ("destro canto" = right flank/corner).
segments.purgatorio-3-g106: "sì che" introduces the result clause "so that"; "l'ombra era da me a la grotta" = "the shadow was from me to the cave" (= lay between me and the cave). "Era" is imperfect (descriptive/past state).
segments.purgatorio-3-g107: "restaro" is an archaic passato remoto (they remained/stopped) used in narrative: they halted/paused.
segments.purgatorio-3-g108: "e trasser sé in dietro alquanto," — "trasser" is a narrative past form of "trarre" (they drew), with reflexive "sé" meaning "they drew themselves back"; "in dietro alquanto" = "backwards a little."
segments.purgatorio-3-g109: "e tutti li altri che venieno appresso," = "and all the others who were coming after"; "venieno" is an archaic imperfect/past form of "venire" (were coming).
segments.purgatorio-3-g110: "non sappiendo 'l perché," — "sappiendo" is a present participle/gerund from "sapere" used here to mean "not knowing"; "'l perché" = "the reason/why"; the phrase means "not knowing why."
segments.purgatorio-3-g111: "fenno altrettanto." — "fenno" is an archaic passato remoto of "fare" (they did/made), here meaning "they did the same"; "altrettanto" = "likewise/just as much."
segments.purgatorio-3-g112: "Sanza" is an archaic form of "senza" (without); "vostra domanda" = "your question/asking"; "io vi confesso" = "I confess to you" (present indicative, with dative clitic "vi"); "che questo è corpo uman che voi vedete" is the content clause: "that this is a human body which you see" — "uman" is the archaic form of "umano."
segments.purgatorio-3-g113: "per che" introduces the logical conclusion "therefore/hence"; "'l lume del sole in terra è fesso" = "the light of the sun on the earth is split/cleft" — "è" present of "essere" and "fesso" an adjectival/predicative past participle meaning "split/broken," describing the light as broken on the ground.

sentences.purgatorio-3-s9: Now, if nothing casts a shadow before me, do not be more astonished than at the heavens, which do not let one ray encumber another.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s10: To endure torments — the hot and the frosts — Virtue provides similar bodies to do so; and, as it acts, it does not wish to reveal itself to us.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s11: Mad is he who hopes that our reason can traverse the infinite way that holds one substance in three persons.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s12: Be content, human people, with the 'quia'; for if you had been able to see everything, there would have been no need for Mary to give birth; and you saw desires without fruit so that their desire would have been quieted, which is given to them eternally as mourning: I speak of Aristotle and Plato and many others;
sentences.purgatorio-3-s13: We meanwhile came to the foot of the mountain; there we found the rock so steep that ready legs would be useless there.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s14: Between Lerice and Turbia the most deserted, the most broken ruin is a staircase, and toward that (side) it is easy and open.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s15: 'Now who knows from which side the slope descends,' my master said, stopping his pace, 'so that whoever goes without a wing may be able to climb?'
sentences.purgatorio-3-s17: And while he, keeping his face low, was weighing in his mind the course of the path, and I looked upward around the rock, on the left hand there appeared to me a company of souls who were moving their feet toward us, and it did not seem that they were coming slowly.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s18: "Lift your eyes, master," I said; "here on this side is someone who will give us counsel, if you cannot get it from yourself."
sentences.purgatorio-3-s19: He then looked, and with a composed bearing replied, "Let's go over there, for they come slowly; and you steady your hope, sweet son."
sentences.purgatorio-3-s20: That crowd was still far off — I mean after our thousand steps, as far as a good hurler would reach with his hand — when they all pressed themselves to the hard boulders of the high bank, and stood firm and close together, as one who goes hesitating would stand.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s5: The sun, which blazed crimson behind, was for me broken when it stood before the figure that had the support of its rays upon me.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s6: I turned to one side, fearful of being abandoned, when I saw the dark land alone before me; and my comfort: 'Why do you still mistrust?'
sentences.purgatorio-3-s7: — and, turning wholly toward me, it began to say: 'Do you not believe that I am with you and that I guide you?'
sentences.purgatorio-3-s8: Evening is already there where the body is buried, within which I used to cast a shadow; Naples has it, and it has been taken by Brandizio.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s37: Now see whether you can make me happy by revealing to my good Constance how you saw me, and also this prohibition; for here, because of those on the other side, much remains.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s1: Although the sudden flight scattered them across the countryside, their faces turned toward the mountain where reason probes into it, I drew myself close to my faithful companion: and how would I have run without him?
sentences.purgatorio-3-s2: Who would have drawn me up the mountain?
sentences.purgatorio-3-s3: He seemed to me remorseful of himself: or a dignified, clear conscience — how that small fault bites you like a bitter pang!
sentences.purgatorio-3-s4: When his feet left off their haste, which takes away honor from every action, my mind, which had been contracted before, widened its intent, as it wanders, and I turned my face toward the hill that spreads itself upward toward the higher heaven.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s29: When I had humbly denied ever having seen him, he said, "Now look," and showed me a wound at the top of his chest.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s30: Then, smiling, he said: "I am Manfred, nephew/descendant of Empress Constance; therefore I beg you that, when you return, go to my fair daughter, mother of the honor of Sicily and of Aragon, and tell her the truth, if anything else is said."
sentences.purgatorio-3-s31: After my body had been broken by two mortal points, I gave myself up, weeping, to the One who willingly forgives.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s32: My sins were horrible; but infinite goodness has such great arms that it accepts whatever turns itself to it.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s25: Do not be astonished, but believe that it is not without a power coming from heaven that (they) try to overpower this wall.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s26: Thus the master; and that worthy company said, 'Return,' he said, 'enter forward then,' making a sign with the backs of their hands.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s27: And one of them began: 'Whoever you are, as you go, turn your face; take heed whether you ever saw me there.'
sentences.purgatorio-3-s28: I turned toward him and fixed my gaze on him: he was blond and handsome and of noble appearance, but one of his eyebrows had been split by a blow.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s33: If the shepherd of Cosenza, who was set to hunt me by Clement then, had well read this face in God, the bones of my body would still be in the hollow of the bridge near Benevento, under the custody of the stern Moorish woman.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s34: Now the rain wets them and the wind moves them from outside the realm, almost along the Verde, where he removed/changed them by extinguished light (i.e. under cover of darkness).
sentences.purgatorio-3-s35: By their curse eternal love is not so lost that it cannot return, so long as hope still has the green bud.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s36: It is true that whoever dies in contumacy against the Holy Church, even if at the end he repents, must remain off this bank for every period that he has been in his presumption — thirty (years) — unless such a decree is made shorter by good prayers.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s21: “O well-finished, O already-elected spirits,” Virgil began, “for that peace which I believe awaits you all, tell us where the mountain lies, so that it may be possible to go upward; for wasting time is all the more displeasing to whoever knows more.”
sentences.purgatorio-3-s22: As the little sheep come out of the fold one by one, two by two, three by three, the others stand shyly, lowering their eyes and their muzzles; and what the first does the others do, leaning against her if she halts, simple and quiet and not knowing the reason; thus I saw the head of that fortunate flock begin to move to come together, modest in countenance and upright in its bearing.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s23: As those in front saw the light broken on the ground from my right side — so that the shadow lay between me and the cave — they stopped, and drew themselves back a little; and all the others who were coming after, not knowing why, did the same.
sentences.purgatorio-3-s24: Without your asking, I confess to you that this is a human body which you see; therefore the sunlight on earth is broken.
